title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Overview
This website looks at the Cambridge Central School District (Cambridge CSD, Washington County, New York) alongside nearby districts, using public data from the New York State Education Department (NYSED) and selected other sources. It is one of several analyses in the ccs project; the aim is to put Cambridge‚Äôs numbers in context rather than to view them in isolation.

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Comparison groups
Different questions call for different peer sets, so this website uses two groups that can be adjusted as the work develops:

Graph group (5 districts) ‚Äî used for charts, where a short list keeps lines legible: Cambridge, Greenwich, Salem (all Washington County), Schuylerville (Saratoga County), and Hoosick Falls (Rensselaer County). These are nearby small districts.
Table group (12 districts) ‚Äî used for tables, which can hold more rows: every Washington County K-12 district plus the two graph-group districts that sit just outside the county (Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls).

The table group is the broader set; the graph group is a readable subset of it. Putnam CSD is left out of the ‚ÄúWashington County K-12‚Äù set because it is a K-8 district ‚Äì it has no high school of its own and pays to send its high-school students elsewhere, so it is not comparable on K-12 measures.







Group
Districts




Graph (charts)
Cambridge, Greenwich, Hoosick Falls, Salem, Schuylerville


Table (tables)
The graph group + Argyle, Fort Ann, Fort Edward, Granville, Hartford, Hudson Falls, Whitehall

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Data and a few cautions
The underlying numbers come from NYSED‚Äôs public downloads and other reputable sources; each chapter and the Sources appendix give the specifics. A few things to keep in mind throughout:

Year labels are school-year-ending years. ‚Äú2025‚Äù means the 2024‚Äì25 school year. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Teacher counts begin in 2017‚Äì18. So time series that depend on teachers (such as students per teacher) start at school-year-ending 2018, even though enrollment runs back to 2015‚Äì16.
Students per teacher is an approximation, not official class size. It divides K-12 enrollment by a district-wide teacher headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents). It is useful for comparison across districts and over time, but it is not NYSED‚Äôs official pupil-teacher or average-class-size figure. See the Sources appendix for detail.
Charter schools are excluded ‚Äî they have no district-total row in the source data ‚Äî so these are district-operated figures.

title: 1¬Ý Students per teacher
text:
1.1 Students per teacher, over time
A natural question is how crowded Cambridge‚Äôs classrooms are compared with nearby districts. The measure most people picture is class size ‚Äî the number of students in a typical classroom ‚Äî but NYSED does not publish class size by district in the data used here. The closest available measure is K-12 students per teacher: a district‚Äôs K-12 enrollment divided by its number of teachers.
Students per teacher is a useful staffing comparison, but it is not class size, and it is always lower than class size ‚Äî because it counts every teacher (including part-time teachers and specialists who don‚Äôt lead a regular class), not just the teachers standing in front of a full classroom. Read it as a staffing ratio, not a count of children in a room.
A reminder from the Overview: the teacher figure is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), so this is an approximate measure for comparing districts and tracking change over time. Lower means fewer students per teacher.
The chart below tracks the graph group from school-year-ending 2018 (the first year teacher counts exist) through 2025.
Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the group.
Code
g = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+
    .select("district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K‚Äì12 students per teacher",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="District K‚Äì12 enrollment √∑ teacher headcount, 2017‚Äì18 to 2024‚Äì25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure¬Ý1.1: K‚Äì12 students per teacher by school year ending, 2018 (2017‚Äì18) through 2025 (2024‚Äì25). Lower means fewer students per teacher. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.
In 2017‚Äì18 only Salem had a lower ratio than Cambridge. Cambridge‚Äôs ratio then drifted down ‚Äî its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% (from 869 to 741) while its teacher headcount stayed roughly flat ‚Äî and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the lowest students-per-teacher ratio of the five. A jump in reported teachers in 2023‚Äì24 pushed its 2024 figure especially low (8.2). Schuylerville and Greenwich actually fell more over the full period (about 2.0 and 1.9, versus Cambridge‚Äôs 1.5), but from much higher starting points, so they remain above Cambridge.

title: 1¬Ý Students per teacher
text:
1.2 The full comparison group
The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2018 to 2025 (a negative value means fewer students per teacher than in 2018). Cambridge is highlighted.


Code
t = cc.panel_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP)

# Long -> wide (years become columns), Washington County districts first,
# then alphabetical within each region.
wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="k12_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns(
    (pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg")
)

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="K‚Äì12 students per teacher",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017‚Äì18) through 2025 (2024‚Äì25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018‚Üí2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="‚Äî")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and "
            "Student & Educator database (teacher counts). "
            "Ratio = district K-12 enrollment √∑ teacher headcount; an "
            "approximate comparison measure, not official class size. "
            "Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25)."
        )
    )
)




Table¬Ý1.1: K‚Äì12 students per teacher by district and school year ending.

Source: NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and Student & Educator database (teacher counts). Ratio = district K-12 enrollment √∑ teacher headcount; an approximate comparison measure, not official class size. Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25).












Across the wider group, Cambridge ends among the lowest ratios ‚Äî close to Salem ‚Äî while the rest of Washington County‚Äôs K-12 districts span a wider range. The table is also a reminder that these districts differ greatly in size (compare small districts like Fort Edward with larger ones like Hudson Falls and Granville), which shapes how much a single hire or a small enrollment swing moves the ratio.

title: 1¬Ý Students per teacher
section: 1.3 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
text:
1.3 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
The ratio above has two limitations worth testing. NYSED reports a teacher headcount, whereas the standard pupil-teacher ratio ‚Äî the one NCES, the OECD and most cross-state comparisons use ‚Äî divides enrollment by teacher full-time-equivalents (FTE). And it comes from a single state source. The federal Common Core of Data (CCD) offers an independent check: it reports teacher FTE and enrollment for every district. (CCD labels a school year by its fall term, so its years are shifted here to match NYSED‚Äôs school-year-ending labels.)
First the diagnostic question ‚Äî do the two measures agree for Cambridge?
The two measures never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both fall sharply in 2023‚Äì24.


Code
ny = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)
    .select("year_end", pl.col("k12_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NYSED (teacher headcount)").alias("source"))
)
ccd_cam = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter((pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS) & (pl.col("year_end") >= 2018))
    .select("year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NCES (teacher FTE)").alias("source"))
)
both = pl.concat(
    [
        ny.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
        ccd_cam.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
    ]
)
source_colors = {
    "NYSED (teacher headcount)": "#C8102E",
    "NCES (teacher FTE)": "#2C6E9B",
}

(
    ggplot(both, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="source"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=source_colors, name="")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher",
        title="Cambridge students per teacher: headcount vs. FTE",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure¬Ý1.2: Cambridge students per teacher: NYSED (teacher headcount) vs.¬ÝNCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE), by school year ending. Sources: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases; NCES CCD via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.





The two lines stay close because, in small districts, a teacher headcount and its full-time-equivalent are nearly the same ‚Äî there are few part-timers. More important, the independent FTE measure shows the same sharp decline in 2023‚Äì24, so that turning point is not an artifact of how NYSED counts teachers.
Having confirmed the two agree, the next chart and table repeat the earlier views using the NCES FTE-based ratio ‚Äî the version directly comparable to other states and to national figures.
On the NCES measure too, Cambridge has the lowest ratio of the group in the two most recent years.


Code
gc = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2018)
    .select("district", "year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge_ccd = gc.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(gc, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge_ccd, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher (NCES FTE)",
        title="District enrollment √∑ teacher FTE (NCES CCD), 2017‚Äì18 to 2024‚Äì25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure¬Ý1.3: NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by school year ending. Source: NCES Common Core of Data via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.







Code
tc = cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2018, 2025))

wide_c = (
    tc.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="ccd_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols_c = [c for c in wide_c.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide_c = wide_c.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide_c, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Students per teacher (NCES FTE basis)",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017‚Äì18) through 2025 (2024‚Äì25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols_c + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols_c)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018‚Üí2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="‚Äî")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE and enrollment), "
            "via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal. Ratio = district "
            "enrollment √∑ teacher full-time-equivalents ‚Äî the nationally "
            "standard pupil-teacher ratio. CCD years are shifted to "
            "school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25)."
        )
    )
)




Table¬Ý1.2: NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by district and school year ending.

Source: NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE and enrollment), via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal. Ratio = district enrollment √∑ teacher full-time-equivalents ‚Äî the nationally standard pupil-teacher ratio. CCD years are shifted to school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25).












One difference to keep in mind: the NCES ratio divides total district enrollment (which includes pre-K) by teacher FTE, while the NYSED ratio earlier uses K-12 enrollment and a headcount. The two therefore differ slightly in level, but they agree on the pattern ‚Äî which is what the comparison turns on.

title: 2¬Ý School district spending
text:
2.1 What ‚Äúspending per pupil‚Äù means here
How much does each district spend to educate a student, and how does Cambridge compare? This chapter opens with the broadest summary ‚Äî current operating spending per pupil ‚Äî and will build toward the major categories of spending in later sections.
A district‚Äôs spending can be measured several ways, and the choice changes the number a lot. This chapter uses current operating spending: the cost of running schools in a year ‚Äî salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services, food service, and federally funded programs ‚Äî divided by K-12 enrollment.
It deliberately leaves out two things that make year-to-year comparisons misleading:
This is close to the ‚Äúcurrent spending per pupil‚Äù measure the U.S. Census and the National Center for Education Statistics use to compare districts and states. The dollars come from the New York State Comptroller‚Äôs financial-reporting data and are not adjusted for inflation, so part of the upward drift over time reflects rising prices rather than rising real resources.

title: 2¬Ý School district spending
text:
Capital projects (new buildings, roofs, buses) and the debt that pays for them. These arrive in large, irregular lumps ‚Äî one building project can briefly multiply a district‚Äôs total spending. Salem‚Äôs all-funds spending hit about $87,000 per pupil in 2021‚Äì22 for this reason, against current operating spending of about $26,000 that year.
Transfers between a district‚Äôs own funds, which would otherwise be counted twice.

title: 2¬Ý School district spending
text:
2.2 Spending per pupil, over time
Cambridge spends near the top of the group per pupil ‚Äî consistently second only to Salem.


Code
g = (
    cc.spending_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2016)
    .select("district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "current_pp", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2016, 2026)))
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=_dollars)
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Current operating spending per pupil",
        title="Current operating spending per K-12 pupil, 2015‚Äì16 to 2024‚Äì25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure¬Ý2.1: Current operating spending per K‚Äì12 pupil (nominal dollars) by school year ending, 2016 (2015‚Äì16) through 2025 (2024‚Äì25). Source: NYS Comptroller local-government financial data; K-12 enrollment from NYSED.





The two highest-spending districts per pupil ‚Äî Cambridge and Salem ‚Äî are also the two smallest by enrollment. That is the cost-side mirror of the students-per-teacher picture: a small district spreads fixed costs (a principal, a building, a bus run) over fewer students, so its spending per pupil runs high even when its total budget is modest. Every district‚Äôs figure rises over the decade, but remember these are nominal dollars ‚Äî much of that climb is inflation.

title: 2¬Ý School district spending
text:
2.3 The full comparison group
The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2016 to 2025 (in nominal dollars). Cambridge is highlighted.


Code
t = cc.spending_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2016, 2025))

wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="current_pp", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2016")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Current operating spending per pupil",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2016 (2015‚Äì16) through 2025 (2024‚Äì25), nominal dollars",
    )
    .fmt_currency(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=0)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2016‚Üí2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="‚Äî")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** New York State Comptroller local-government financial "
            "data (expenditures); K-12 enrollment from NYSED. Current operating "
            "spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding "
            "capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal "
            "dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25)."
        )
    )
)




Table¬Ý2.1: Current operating spending per K‚Äì12 pupil by district and school year ending (nominal dollars).

Source: New York State Comptroller local-government financial data (expenditures); K-12 enrollment from NYSED. Current operating spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024‚Äì25).












Across the wider group the same pattern holds: the smallest districts tend to post the highest spending per pupil, and Cambridge sits near the top. Later sections will open up what the money buys ‚Äî the major categories of spending ‚Äî and add the general-fund-only and total-all-funds views for context.

title: Sources & references
text:
NYSED data files
All figures on this website come from public data. This appendix documents each source, notes the main caveats, and lists full references (APA style) at the end. Citation keys are given in brackets so they can be reused in the text.
The enrollment and staffing numbers are built from two New York State Education Department databases, both downloaded from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php and distributed as annual Microsoft Access files:
These were stacked into a district-by-year panel by src/build_enrollment_teachers.py; the dataset‚Äôs own README records the build in full.
Caveats worth repeating. num_teachers is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), while k12_enrollment is K-12 only. The derived students-per-teacher ratio is therefore an approximate measure for comparison across districts and over time ‚Äî not NYSED‚Äôs official pupil-teacher ratio or average class size. Charter schools are excluded (they have no district-total row), and a small number of districts reorganized within the window (statewide, not in this comparison group).

title: Sources & references
text:
Enrollment database (New York State Education Department, 2025a) ‚Äî the BEDS Day Enrollment table, which gives K-12 enrollment by district. Coverage on this website: school years 2015‚Äì16 through 2024‚Äì25. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Student & Educator (‚ÄúSTUDED‚Äù) database (New York State Education Department, 2025b) ‚Äî the Staff table, which gives teacher, principal, counselor, and social-worker counts plus teacher attendance and turnover. Coverage: school years 2017‚Äì18 through 2024‚Äì25. Because teacher counts start in 2017‚Äì18, any measure that uses teachers (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending

title: Sources & references
text:
Cambridge CSD annual financial reports
Cambridge‚Äôs audited annual financial statements, for fiscal years ended June 30, 2020 through June 30, 2025, were retrieved from the Federal Audit Clearinghouse (https://app.fac.gov/). The clearinghouse was operated by the U.S. Census Bureau through about 2022 and by the U.S. General Services Administration from 2023 onward ‚Äî which is why the earlier files (FY2020‚ÄìFY2022) carry a CENSUS tag and bear the title ‚ÄúFinancial statements and independent auditor‚Äôs report,‚Äù while the later files (FY2023‚ÄìFY2025) carry a GSAFAC tag and the title ‚ÄúFinancial report.‚Äù They are the same series of district audits.

FY2020 (Cambridge Central School District, 2020), FY2021 (Cambridge Central School District, 2021), FY2022 (Cambridge Central School District, 2022)
FY2023 (Cambridge Central School District, 2023), FY2024 (Cambridge Central School District, 2024), FY2025 (Cambridge Central School District, 2025)

title: Sources & references
text:
Reader comments
Each page of this website has a comment area at the bottom, powered by Giscus. To leave a comment or a reaction, sign in with a free GitHub account; comments are stored as discussions in this website‚Äôs GitHub repository.
